answer:
The directly opposed cosmic frame-verses:

Andreas (Cynewulf, ch.1, p-868413, the opening): 'We have heard of heroes in ages past, / of twelve true thanes under the turning stars, / the Lord's own champions. Their glory lived on / in the grim struggle where standards clashed. / Their lots were laid out as the Lord Himself, / Heaven's High King, handed them their fates.' The cosmic frame: a single Lord allots the lots; history is the shape of His twelve champions enforcing that allotment against the disorder of the Mermedonians (whose 'law' p-471d51 is a parody — the cup that steals 'a man's senses, his soul's true thought'). The world is a stage on which Christ's order is fought into place against pagan custom.

Manu, chapter 1, p-fcafde (Mn_1.81): 'In the Krita age, Dharma is four-footed and complete, and so is truth; no gain comes to men through any unrighteousness.' Then p-1365cd (Mn_1.82): 'In the other ages, Dharma is successively deprived of one foot due to what is gained; and through theft, falsehood, and deceit, Dharma diminishes by one foot in each.' And p-8709d8 (Mn_1.86): 'In the Krita age, the highest Dharma is tapas; in the Treta, knowledge is declared to be so; in the Dvapara, they call it the yajña; and in the Kali age, giving is the one Dharma.' The cosmic frame: dharma is a four-footed reality that loses a foot each yuga; what looks like history is the cyclical unfolding-and-decay of an unchanging order.

Which would survive translation into the other's idiom?

Manu's cyclical frame would translate INTO Andreas's idiom only as a heresy. To say 'the Lord's order has progressively lost three of its four feet, and we are the diminished age' is to make God change with time, which Andreas's God cannot. Manu's frame would survive translation only by becoming a Fall narrative — an Edenic Krita-age followed by progressive decline — which is exactly what early Christian apologists did with their pagan sources. The four feet would have to become four eras leading to the Last Days; cyclicity would have to become eschatology.

Andreas's frame, conversely, would translate INTO Manu's idiom much more easily. Christ's twelve thanes carrying their Lord's order against the Mermedonian-custom is structurally a kshatra-doing-its-dharma in a corrupt age — the kshatriya's role in Mn_1.89 (p-a09d4d) is precisely 'the protection of the people'. Andreas would survive translation as the Kali-age narrative of dharma-keeping kshatriyas under the diminished single-foot of giving (Mn_1.86). The Lord's allotted-lots become the dharmic role-prescription, the twelve become the keepers of the last foot, and the cannibal land becomes the unrighteousness through which dharma is being lost.

Verdict: Andreas's frame survives the move to Manu's idiom (it becomes a kshatra-dharma narrative in the Kali yuga). Manu's frame survives the move to Andreas's idiom only by ceasing to be cyclical — i.e. by becoming Christian eschatology rather than Hindu cosmology. The cyclical frame is the one that does not translate.

tool_calls.result_summary: p-868413 = the framing matla of Andreas; p-471d51 = Mermedonian counter-frame
